summary:
The presence of black deposits on the conjunctiva in patients with glaucoma is most commonly attributed to pigment dispersion syndrome, wherein melanin particles from the iris enter the aqueous humor and can accumulate at the limbal region. This phenomenon occurs naturally within certain types of primary open-angle glaucomas rather than resulting from pharmacological agents such as prostaglandin analogs, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, epinephrine, or beta-blockers—none of which have been shown by available evidence to cause such deposits.  

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{Not specified}}$
